original_abstract: Solar cells incorporating alkyl ammonium metal halides as light absorbers offer a compelling blend of high power conversion efficiency and simplified fabrication, positioning them as a leading candidate for cost-effective thin-film photovoltaics. Despite their potential, these devices typically suffer from significant degradation under prolonged solar exposure. In this work, we demonstrate that this degradation is quasi-reversible and can be substantially mitigated by optimizing the solar cell’s operating environment. Through electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) conducted on devices featuring methylammonium (MA), formamidinium (FA), and mixed-cation $MA_xFA_{1-x}$ lead triiodide active layers, we identify distinct kinetic behaviors. Variable-temperature EIS reveals that the diffusion coefficient of MA ions is significantly higher than that of FA ions. Furthermore, powder X-ray diffraction (PXRD) analysis of $MAPbI_3$ indicates a structural phase transition and lattice expansion at standard operating temperatures. Based on these findings, we propose that the accelerated degradation of $MAPbI_3$ under sunlight is driven by thermally activated ion transport coupled with a lattice-expanding phase transition—both of which are triggered by the infrared component of the solar spectrum. By applying these insights, we demonstrate that device stability and operational lifetimes are dramatically enhanced when using white-light LEDs, infrared-cutoff filters, or active cooling systems.
humanized_abstract: Recyclability and Enhanced Stability of Perovskite Solar Cells via Light Optimization Alkyl ammonium metal halides-based solar cells have drawn increasing attention owing to their high power conversion efficiency (PCE) and low preparation complexity, which suggest that perovskite solar cells are a promising candidate for commercialization as low-cost thin-film photovoltaics (PVs). Unfortunately, the stability of most perovskite solar cells under sunlight illumination remains a significant challenge, and it remains unclear how to effectively enhance their stability. Herein, we discover that the stability degradation under illumination is quasi-reversible and is highly dependent on the operating conditions of solar cells. Using various measurements including electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) on the methylammonium (MA) based and formamidinium (FA) based lead triiodide perovskite solar cells, as well as on the mixed-cation $MA_xFA_{1-x}$, we investigated the dynamic behavior of these devices and uncovered their underlying physical mechanisms. Variable-temperature EIS experiments were conducted on the $MA_xFA_{1-x}$ PSCs, from which the diffusion coefficients of the MA and FA ions were obtained. Powder X-ray diffraction (PXRD) experiments showed that the $MAPbI_3$ undergoes structural phase transition and exhibits lattice expansion at the standard operating temperature. Thus, based on our experimental results, the rapid stability degradation of $MAPbI_3$ under the illumination is ascribed to the thermal activation of the ions movement and the lattice expanding phase transition triggered by the infrared (IR) part of the solar spectrum. The stability of solar cells can be highly improved using a white-light LEDs, an infrared-cutoff filter, or even a cooling system.
